one_line_takeaway: A survey/taxonomy paper (not original research) that organizes wireless-network forensics into a physical/device/network/cross-layer evidence taxonomy and a readiness-acquisition-correlation-reporting workflow, contrasting traditional and AI-assisted methods using numbers drawn entirely from cited primary studies rather than its own experiments.
key_finding: The paper's central claim (via Table 1) is a coverage gap: prior digital/network/cloud forensics surveys ([10]-[16]) and IoT/mobile security surveys ([6],[17]-[19]) do not treat radio/channel measurements as first-class evidence or model calibration uncertainty, while wireless-security surveys ([20]-[24]) focus on classification robustness/detection accuracy rather than accountable reconstruction; this survey positions itself as the only one addressing all three (wireless evidence, uncertainty/calibration, forensic reconstruction) simultaneously. All numeric results quoted throughout (e.g., a physical-layer backdoor trigger reported at 99.2% attack success with <0.6% clean-accuracy drop [38]; 2.26 picosecond synchronization precision via wireless two-way time transfer [40]; CSI-based drone detection reaching 95.65% true-positive rate at 5 m degrading to 60% at 7 m [85]; on-device multiclass attack recognition at 99.60% accuracy on CICIoT2023 and 99.98% on IoT-23 [88]; 46% reduction in transmitted geolocation digits with zero verification error over 100 simulated flight paths [86]; flow-record matching throughput rising from 2.5 to 9.5 Gbit/s [57]; INT telemetry selection keeping monitoring accuracy above 95% while cutting bandwidth ~40% [65]; a physical-layer covert-tag authentication scheme reaching 98% identification at -15 dB SNR [70]; adversarial phase-flipping augmentation improving robustness by 18.12% against Carlini-Wagner attacks [78]) are results the survey reports from the individual cited primary-source papers -- they are not experiments run by this survey's own authors.
core_contribution: This is a survey/taxonomy paper (ACM Computing Surveys), not an empirical research contribution. Its own contribution (Section 1.3) is: (1) a unified wireless-evidence taxonomy spanning physical-layer, device-layer, network-layer, and cross-layer forensics, explicitly treating radio waveforms and channel measurements as first-class forensic artifacts with documented uncertainty/calibration; (2) a forensic workflow decomposition into readiness & preservation-by-design, acquisition, correlation & analysis, and reporting & reproducibility, with commentary on where AI-assisted methods can safely be inserted at each stage; (3) a review of application areas (anomaly discovery, attribution, provenance/localization, authenticity verification, timeline reconstruction); and (4) a positioning table (Table 1) comparing this survey against 15 prior surveys ([10]-[24]) on three axes -- wireless evidence, uncertainty/calibration, forensic reconstruction -- where this survey claims to be the only one checked on all three.
threat_model: No single threat model of its own -- as a survey the paper aggregates threat models and adversarial-manipulation vectors reported in the many primary studies it cites (per forensic layer): physical-layer waveform manipulation/impersonation/signature forgery and learning-pipeline poisoning of RF classifiers; device-layer SIM/baseband log evasion and baseband state desynchronization via malformed paging/RRC messages; network-layer traffic obfuscation, control-plane deception, and backdoor poisoning of flow classifiers; cross-layer generative feature fabrication (e.g., GAN-mimicked physical-layer fingerprints) and adversarial evasion against fusion models. These are all attributed to the cited primary-source papers (e.g., [38], [50], [62], [76]), not original attacks devised by the survey's authors.
limitations: As a survey, the paper presents no original experiments, datasets, or baselines of its own; every quantitative figure and dataset in the text is secondary, drawn from a cited primary study, so its evidentiary strength depends entirely on the accuracy of those citations. The taxonomy and workflow framing are descriptive/organizational rather than validated (no evaluation of whether the proposed workflow actually improves forensic outcomes in practice). The self-positioning claim in Table 1 (uniquely covering all three criteria) is a qualitative, author-assigned scoring of competing surveys rather than an objective or reproducible metric.

datasets_used: None used directly by this survey -- CICIoT2023 and IoT-23 are datasets used in a cited primary study [88] on on-device multiclass attack recognition, reported second-hand in this survey's Table 2/Section 3.1
quantitative_results: 99.2% attack success rate / <0.6% clean-accuracy degradation for a physical-layer backdoor trigger -- from cited work [38], not this survey's own result
2.26 picosecond timing precision via wireless two-way time transfer synchronization -- from cited work [40]
96.8% (PS module) and 93.5% (TFD module) true positive rates at 10 m, 95.65% overall system TPR at 5 m dropping to 60% at 7 m for CSI-based radio-silent drone detection -- from cited work [85]
Up to 46% reduction in transmitted geolocation digits with zero verification error across 100 simulated flight paths, using a 15 s sampling baseline -- from cited work [86]
99.60% multiclass accuracy on CICIoT2023 and 99.98% on IoT-23 for on-device attack recognition -- from cited work [88]
84.68% minimum accuracy maintained under a 1 KB/s storage limit and 10% CPU budget with 30 active tasks on a Raspberry Pi 3B+ -- from cited work [89]
Flow-record matching throughput increase from 2.5 Gbit/s to 9.5 Gbit/s (Snort-rule-to-IPFIX compilation) -- from cited work [57]
Monitoring accuracy maintained above 95% with ~40% bandwidth reduction via 0-1 Knapsack telemetry-item selection -- from cited work [65]
98% identification rate at -15 dB SNR for a physical-layer covert authentication tag scheme -- from cited work [70]
18.12% classification-accuracy improvement against Carlini-Wagner adversarial attacks via phase-flipping augmentation and soft-label training -- from cited work [78]
SDN-based triggered evidence collection reported at about 97.3% accuracy/F1 -- from cited work [84]
baselines_compared: Not an empirical baseline comparison -- Table 1 qualitatively scores this survey against 15 prior review papers ([10]-[24]) on three criteria (wireless evidence coverage, uncertainty/calibration treatment, forensic-reconstruction focus), using checkmark/partial/cross ratings assigned by the authors rather than a quantitative benchmark
